title_en: Scholarly Method and Structure of Al-Minhaj
content_en:
Al-Minhaj Sharh Sahih Muslim follows the standard structure of a classical Arabic commentary: an-Nawawi proceeds hadith by hadith through Imam Muslim's collection, providing for each a multi-layered analysis that touches on language, law, theology, and spiritual significance as appropriate. The commentary's organization mirrors that of the underlying Sahih Muslim, which is arranged by subject into books (kutub) and chapters (abwab). Within this structure, an-Nawawi can be extremely brief on hadiths he considers straightforward and extensively analytical on hadiths that raise difficult questions.

The linguistic dimension of an-Nawawi's commentary is consistently excellent. He was one of the finest Arabic linguists of the medieval period, and his explanations of rare vocabulary, unusual grammatical constructions, and the precise semantic range of key terms are widely acknowledged as among the clearest and most reliable available. Students of Arabic who use Al-Minhaj as a study text find that it simultaneously advances their command of the language and their understanding of the hadith content, since an-Nawawi's linguistic explanations are always in service of better understanding the intended meaning of the prophetic statements.

The legal dimension of the commentary reflects an-Nawawi's thorough command of Shafi'i jurisprudence and his systematic knowledge of the positions of the other major legal schools. He regularly notes the implications of each hadith for legal rulings and presents the positions of the Shafi'i, Hanafi, Maliki, and Hanbali schools with remarkable precision and fairness. This comparative dimension makes Al-Minhaj genuinely useful to students of all four schools, not only to Shafi'is, since it provides a clear and reliable account of cross-school disagreements and their textual bases.

The theological dimension of the commentary is handled with characteristic Ash'ari care. When hadiths relate to divine attributes, the nature of God's knowledge and power, or the conditions of the afterlife, an-Nawawi addresses these matters in ways consistent with the Ash'ari theological tradition to which he belonged. His discussions of the attributes of God in particular reflect a careful navigation between anthropomorphism and excessive metaphorical interpretation, a balance that has been influential in subsequent Ash'ari scholarship.
